topics.title: Setting Up Rust
topics.description: Install the Rust toolchain with rustup and get your development environment ready to write your first Rust program.

lessons.title: Why Rust?
lessons.body: Rust is a systems programming language that guarantees memory safety without a garbage collector. It is fast, reliable, and productive. Before writing any code you need to install the Rust toolchain on your machine.

lessons.title: Installing rustup
lessons.body: rustup is the official installer and version manager for Rust. On macOS or Linux open a terminal and run the command below. On Windows download rustup-init.exe from rustup.rs. This command downloads a script from the official Rust site. You can visit https://sh.rustup.rs in your browser first to review it. After installation restart your terminal so the PATH changes take effect.
lessons.code:
curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh
# Follow the prompts and choose the default installation
# Then reload your shell:
source $HOME/.cargo/env

lessons.title: Verifying the installation
lessons.body: After installing rustup you should have three tools available: rustc (the compiler), cargo (the build system and package manager), and rustup itself. Run the commands below to verify everything is working.
lessons.code:
rustc --version
# rustc 1.XX.0 (abc1234 2024-XX-XX)

cargo --version
# cargo 1.XX.0 (abc1234 2024-XX-XX)

rustup --version
# rustup 1.XX.0 (abc1234 2024-XX-XX)

lessons.title: Editor support
lessons.body: You can write Rust in any text editor. Popular choices include VS Code with the rust-analyzer extension, IntelliJ with the Rust plugin, and Neovim with LSP support. The key tool is rust-analyzer which provides autocomplete, inline errors, and refactoring. Pick whatever editor you are comfortable with and install rust-analyzer for the best experience. For a deeper dive, check the official Rust Book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) chapter on Getting Started. Also explore the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Hello, Rust!
topics.description: Write and run your very first Rust program using fn main and the println! macro.

lessons.title: Creating a project with Cargo
lessons.body: Cargo is the Rust build tool and package manager. Use cargo new to scaffold a new project. This creates a directory with a Cargo.toml manifest and a src/main.rs file containing a hello world program.
lessons.code:
cargo new hello_rust
cd hello_rust
# Created files:
# Cargo.toml   - project metadata
# src/main.rs  - entry point

lessons.title: The main function
lessons.body: Every Rust executable starts at the main function. It takes no arguments and returns nothing by default. The fn keyword declares a function. Curly braces delimit the function body.

lessons.title: println! is a macro
lessons.body: The exclamation mark after println tells you it is a macro, not a regular function. Macros can accept a variable number of arguments and generate code at compile time. println! prints text to the terminal followed by a newline. You can embed values using curly brace placeholders.

lessons.title: Building and running
lessons.body: Use cargo run to compile and execute your program in one step. Cargo places the compiled binary in the target/debug directory. Use cargo build --release when you want an optimized binary for production. For a deeper dive, check the official Rust Book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) chapter on Hello, World!. Also explore the recommended books in the app for more insights.
lessons.code:
cargo run
# Compiling hello_rust v0.1.0
# Running `target/debug/hello_rust`
# Hello, Rust!

topics.title: Variables and Mutability
topics.description: Learn how Rust handles variables with let bindings, mutability with mut, and the powerful concept of shadowing.

lessons.title: Immutable by default
lessons.body: In Rust variables are immutable by default. Once you bind a value with let you cannot change it. This design choice helps prevent bugs by making mutation an explicit decision rather than the default.

lessons.title: Making variables mutable
lessons.body: Add the mut keyword after let to make a variable mutable. Now you can reassign it as many times as you like. Use mut only when you genuinely need to change a value.

lessons.title: Shadowing
lessons.body: You can declare a new variable with the same name using let again. This is called shadowing. The new binding shadows the old one. Unlike mut, shadowing lets you change the type of the value while reusing the name.

lessons.title: Constants
lessons.body: Use const for values that never change and are known at compile time. Constants must have a type annotation and use SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE by convention. Unlike let bindings, constants can be declared in any scope including the global scope. They cannot use mut and must be set to a constant expression. For a deeper dive, check the official Rust Book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) chapter on Variables and Mutability. Also explore the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Scalar Data Types
topics.description: Explore Rust's four scalar types: integers, floating-point numbers, booleans, and characters.

lessons.title: Integer types
lessons.body: Rust has signed and unsigned integers in various sizes. Signed types (i8, i16, i32, i64, i128) can hold negative values. Unsigned types (u8, u16, u32, u64, u128) hold only non-negative values. The default integer type is i32. You can use underscores in numeric literals for readability.

lessons.title: Floating-point types
lessons.body: Rust has two floating-point types: f32 (single precision) and f64 (double precision). The default is f64 because on modern CPUs it is roughly the same speed as f32 but has more precision. Use f32 only when you have a specific need such as GPU programming or memory constraints.

lessons.title: Booleans
lessons.body: The bool type has exactly two values: true and false. Booleans are one byte in size. They are the result of comparison operators and are used heavily in control flow such as if expressions and while loops.

lessons.title: Characters
lessons.body: The char type represents a single Unicode scalar value. It is four bytes in size, so it can represent much more than just ASCII. Use single quotes for char literals and double quotes for string literals. Chars can represent letters, digits, emoji, and many other Unicode symbols. For a deeper dive, check the official Rust Book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) chapter on Data Types. Also explore the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Compound Types
topics.description: Group multiple values together using tuples and arrays, Rust's two primitive compound types.

lessons.title: Tuples
lessons.body: A tuple groups a fixed number of values with potentially different types into one compound value. Once declared a tuple cannot grow or shrink. You access elements by index using a dot followed by the position starting at zero.

lessons.title: Destructuring tuples
lessons.body: You can unpack a tuple into individual variables using pattern matching. This is called destructuring. It is a clean way to extract all values at once instead of accessing them by index.

lessons.title: Arrays
lessons.body: An array is a fixed-length collection where every element has the same type. Arrays are stack-allocated and very fast. Use square brackets for the type annotation: [type; length]. Access elements with index notation. Rust checks array bounds at runtime and panics on out-of-bounds access.

lessons.title: When to use each
lessons.body: Use tuples when you need to group a small number of values of different types, such as returning multiple values from a function. Use arrays when you have a fixed number of values of the same type, like days of the week or a buffer. If you need a growable collection use Vec instead, which you will learn about later. For a deeper dive, check the official Rust Book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) chapter on Data Types. Also explore the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Functions and Return Values
topics.description: Define reusable blocks of code with functions, pass parameters, and return values using Rust's expression-based syntax.

lessons.title: Declaring functions
lessons.body: Use the fn keyword to declare a function. Rust uses snake_case for function names by convention. You can define functions before or after main because Rust does not care about declaration order within a file.

lessons.title: Parameters
lessons.body: Functions can accept parameters. Each parameter must have a type annotation. Separate multiple parameters with commas. When you call the function you pass arguments that match the declared types.

lessons.title: Return values
lessons.body: Specify a return type with an arrow (->). In Rust the last expression in a function body is the return value automatically. You do not need a return keyword or a semicolon on the final expression. Adding a semicolon turns an expression into a statement, which returns the unit type () instead.

lessons.title: Early returns
lessons.body: Use the return keyword to exit a function early. This is useful inside conditionals when you want to bail out before reaching the end. The return keyword requires a semicolon. For the final expression you can omit both return and the semicolon. For a deeper dive, check the official Rust Book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) chapter on Functions. Also explore the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Comments and Documentation
topics.description: Write clear code comments and generate beautiful documentation with Rust's built-in doc comment system.

lessons.title: Line comments
lessons.body: Use double slashes to write a line comment. Everything after // on that line is ignored by the compiler. Line comments are the most common comment style in Rust. Place them above or beside the code they describe.

lessons.title: Block comments
lessons.body: Use /* and */ for block comments that span multiple lines. Block comments can be nested in Rust, which is unusual compared to most languages. However, Rust developers generally prefer line comments.

lessons.title: Doc comments with ///
lessons.body: Triple slashes create documentation comments. They support Markdown formatting and are placed directly above the item they document. Running cargo doc generates HTML documentation from these comments. This is how the Rust standard library is documented.

lessons.title: Module-level doc comments
lessons.body: Use //! to write documentation for the enclosing module or crate. These comments typically go at the top of lib.rs or main.rs and describe the overall purpose of the package. You can generate and view documentation locally with cargo doc --open. For a deeper dive, check the official Rust Book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) chapter on Comments. Also explore the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Control Flow: if and else
topics.description: Make decisions in your code using if, else if, and else expressions, and learn how Rust treats them as expressions that return values.

lessons.title: Basic if expressions
lessons.body: The if keyword evaluates a condition and runs a block of code when the condition is true. Unlike many languages, Rust does not require parentheses around the condition. The condition must be a bool. Rust will not automatically convert numbers or other types to booleans.

lessons.title: else and else if
lessons.body: Use else to run code when the condition is false. Chain multiple conditions with else if. Rust evaluates conditions from top to bottom and runs only the first matching branch.

lessons.title: if as an expression
lessons.body: In Rust if is an expression, meaning it returns a value. You can assign the result of an if expression to a variable. Both branches must return the same type. This replaces the ternary operator found in other languages.

lessons.title: Combining conditions
lessons.body: Use logical operators to combine conditions. The && operator means and, the || operator means or, and the ! operator means not. You can group conditions with parentheses for clarity. Keep conditions readable and consider extracting complex logic into well-named variables. For a deeper dive, check the official Rust Book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) chapter on Control Flow. Also explore the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Loops in Rust
topics.description: Repeat actions with Rust's three loop constructs: loop for infinite loops, while for conditional loops, and for for iteration.

lessons.title: The loop keyword
lessons.body: The loop keyword creates an infinite loop. Use break to exit and continue to skip to the next iteration. You can return a value from a loop by placing it after break. This makes loop an expression, which is unique to Rust.

lessons.title: while loops
lessons.body: A while loop runs as long as its condition is true. The condition is checked before each iteration. Use while when you do not know how many times you need to loop but you have a clear stopping condition.

lessons.title: for loops and ranges
lessons.body: The for loop iterates over elements of a collection or a range. It is the most common loop in Rust because it is safe and concise. Ranges use the .. syntax where the end is exclusive, or ..= for inclusive ranges.

lessons.title: Loop labels and nested loops
lessons.body: When you have nested loops you can label them with a tick prefix like 'outer. Then use break 'outer or continue 'outer to control which loop is affected. This avoids the need for boolean flags to break out of nested loops. For a deeper dive, check the official Rust Book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) chapter on Repetition with Loops. Also explore the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Ownership Essentials
topics.description: Understand Rust's ownership model that ensures memory safety at compile time without a garbage collector.

lessons.title: The three ownership rules
lessons.body: Rust enforces three rules at compile time. First, each value has exactly one owner. Second, there can only be one owner at a time. Third, when the owner goes out of scope the value is dropped. These rules eliminate entire classes of bugs like use-after-free and double-free.

lessons.title: Moves
lessons.body: When you assign a heap-allocated value like a String to another variable ownership moves. The original variable becomes invalid and cannot be used. This prevents two variables from freeing the same memory.

lessons.title: Clone for deep copies
lessons.body: If you need two independent copies of a heap-allocated value use the clone method. This creates a full deep copy so both variables are valid. Cloning is explicit because it can be expensive for large data.

lessons.title: Copy types live on the stack
lessons.body: Simple types like integers, floats, booleans, and chars implement the Copy trait. When you assign them the value is copied on the stack automatically. No move happens because copying stack data is cheap. Types that implement Copy include all integer types, bool, f32, f64, char, and tuples of Copy types. For a deeper dive, check the official Rust Book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) chapter on Understanding Ownership. Also explore the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: References and Borrowing
topics.description: Learn how to borrow values without taking ownership using immutable and mutable references.

lessons.title: Immutable references with &
lessons.body: A reference lets you refer to a value without taking ownership. Use the ampersand to create a reference. The original owner keeps the value and the reference can read it. This is called borrowing. You can have multiple immutable references at the same time.

lessons.title: Mutable references with &mut
lessons.body: To modify a borrowed value you need a mutable reference. Use &mut to create one. The original variable must also be declared as mut. Rust allows only one mutable reference to a value at a time to prevent data races at compile time.

lessons.title: The borrowing rules
lessons.body: Rust enforces two rules for references. First, you can have either one mutable reference or any number of immutable references at the same time but not both. Second, references must always be valid. The compiler prevents dangling references at compile time.

lessons.title: References in practice
lessons.body: Borrowing is the preferred way to pass data to functions when you do not need ownership. It avoids unnecessary cloning and makes your code efficient. Most functions should take references rather than owned values. Use &T for read-only access and &mut T only when the function needs to modify the data. For a deeper dive, check the official Rust Book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) chapter on References and Borrowing. Also explore the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: The String Type
topics.description: Understand the difference between String and &str, and learn how to create and manipulate text in Rust.

lessons.title: String vs &str
lessons.body: Rust has two main string types. String is a growable, heap-allocated string that you own. The &str type is a string slice, an immutable reference to a sequence of UTF-8 bytes. String literals like "hello" are &str. Think of String as the owned version and &str as the borrowed view.
lessons.code:
let s1: &str = "hello";           // string literal, stored in binary
let s2: String = String::from("hello"); // heap-allocated, owned
let s3: String = "hello".to_string();   // another way to create String

println!("{} {} {}", s1, s2, s3);

lessons.title: Creating and growing strings
lessons.body: Create an empty String with String::new() and add content with push for a single character or push_str for a string slice. You can also use format! to build strings from multiple values without taking ownership of any of them.

lessons.title: String concatenation
lessons.body: You can concatenate strings using the + operator, but this takes ownership of the left operand. The right operand must be a &str. For joining multiple strings without ownership concerns use format! instead. You can also use the join method on a slice of strings.

lessons.title: Iterating over strings
lessons.body: Rust strings are UTF-8 encoded so you cannot index them with s[0]. Instead use the chars method to iterate over Unicode characters or bytes for raw bytes. The len method returns the byte length, not the character count. Use chars().count() for the number of characters. For a deeper dive, check the official Rust Book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) chapter on Storing UTF-8 Encoded Text with Strings. Also explore the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Type Casting and Conversions
topics.description: Convert between types safely using the as keyword and the From and Into traits.

lessons.title: The as keyword
lessons.body: Use the as keyword for primitive type casting. This works for numeric conversions such as integer to float, widening, and narrowing. Be aware that narrowing conversions (like i32 to u8) can silently truncate values. The as keyword only works with primitive types.

lessons.title: From and Into traits
lessons.body: The From trait provides a standard way to create a type from another type. When you implement From you get the Into trait for free. Use From::from or .into() for conversions. Unlike as, these conversions are type-safe and will not compile if the conversion is not defined.

lessons.title: String conversions
lessons.body: Convert numbers to strings with to_string() or format!(). Parse strings into numbers with the parse method, which returns a Result because the string might not be a valid number. Use the turbofish syntax to specify the target type.

lessons.title: TryFrom for fallible conversions
lessons.body: When a conversion might fail use TryFrom instead of From. It returns a Result so you can handle the error gracefully. For example converting a large i32 into a u8 can fail if the value does not fit. TryFrom makes these edge cases explicit and safe. For a deeper dive, check the official Rust Book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) appendix on Operators and the Rust by Example section on Conversion. Also explore the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Stack vs Heap
topics.description: Understand how Rust uses stack and heap memory and why it matters for performance and ownership.

lessons.title: What is the stack?
lessons.body: The stack is a region of memory that stores data in a last-in first-out order. It is very fast because the CPU just moves a pointer up and down. All data on the stack must have a known fixed size at compile time. Local variables, function parameters, and return addresses live on the stack.

lessons.title: What is the heap?
lessons.body: The heap is a region of memory for data whose size is not known at compile time or that needs to live beyond the current scope. Allocating on the heap is slower because the allocator must find a big enough block. The result is a pointer that lives on the stack and points to the heap data.

lessons.title: Why it matters
lessons.body: Understanding stack vs heap explains why ownership works the way it does. Stack data is cheap to copy so types like i32 and bool implement Copy. Heap data is expensive to copy so String and Vec use move semantics. When a heap-allocated value goes out of scope Rust calls drop to free the heap memory.

lessons.title: Box for heap allocation
lessons.body: Sometimes you want to explicitly put data on the heap. Use Box<T> for this. Box is a smart pointer that owns heap-allocated data and frees it when dropped. Box is useful for large data you do not want on the stack, recursive types, and trait objects. You will use it more as you advance. For a deeper dive, check the official Rust Book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) chapter on What Is Ownership and the section on Using Box. Also explore the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Cargo and Crates
topics.description: Master Cargo, Rust's build system and package manager, and learn how to use external crates in your projects.

lessons.title: Cargo basics
lessons.body: Cargo handles building, testing, and managing dependencies for Rust projects. Key commands include cargo new to create a project, cargo build to compile, cargo run to compile and execute, and cargo test to run tests. The Cargo.toml file is the manifest that describes your project.
lessons.code:
cargo new my_project   # create a new project
cd my_project
cargo build            # compile in debug mode
cargo run              # compile and run
cargo test             # run tests
cargo build --release  # compile with optimizations

lessons.title: Understanding Cargo.toml
lessons.body: Cargo.toml is the configuration file for your project. It has sections for package metadata, dependencies, and build settings. The package section includes the name, version, and edition. The dependencies section lists external crates your project uses.
lessons.code:
[package]
name = "my_project"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2021"

[dependencies]
rand = "0.8"
serde = { version = "1.0", features = ["derive"] }

lessons.title: Adding and using crates
lessons.body: Crates are Rust packages published on crates.io. Add a crate by listing it under dependencies in Cargo.toml or by running cargo add. Then use it in your code with the use keyword. Cargo automatically downloads, compiles, and links the crate.

lessons.title: Project structure and workspaces
lessons.body: A typical Cargo project has src/main.rs for binaries and src/lib.rs for libraries. You can have both in one project. For larger projects use workspaces to manage multiple related packages in one repository. The Cargo.lock file records exact dependency versions to ensure reproducible builds. Commit Cargo.lock for binaries but not for libraries. For a deeper dive, check the official Rust Book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) chapter on Hello, Cargo! and the Cargo Book (doc.rust-lang.org/cargo). Also explore the recommended books in the app for more insights.
lessons.code:
my_project/
  Cargo.toml
  Cargo.lock
  src/
    main.rs    # binary entry point
    lib.rs     # library code
  tests/
    integration_test.rs
  examples/
    demo.rs    # run with: cargo run --example demo

topics.title: Structs & Fields
topics.description: Define custom data types with named fields using Rust structs.

lessons.title: Defining a Struct
lessons.body: A struct groups related values under one name. Each field has a name and a type, separated by a colon. You create an instance by providing values for every field.

lessons.title: Accessing and Mutating Fields
lessons.body: Use dot notation to read or write a field. The entire instance must be declared mut to change any field; Rust does not allow marking individual fields as mutable.

lessons.title: Tuple Structs and Unit Structs
lessons.body: Tuple structs have fields without names, accessed by index. They are useful for lightweight wrappers. Unit structs have no fields at all and are used as markers or for trait implementations.

lessons.title: Struct Update Syntax
lessons.body: The struct update syntax lets you create a new instance by copying most fields from an existing one and overriding specific fields. Use the .. operator followed by the source instance. For a deeper dive, see The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) chapter on structs. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Enums & Variants
topics.description: Model data that can be one of several variants using Rust enums.

lessons.title: Defining an Enum
lessons.body: An enum lets you define a type by listing its possible variants. Each variant is a distinct value of that type. Enums are perfect for representing a fixed set of choices.

lessons.title: Variants with Data
lessons.body: Variants can hold data of different types. A variant can carry unnamed fields like a tuple, named fields like a struct, or no data at all. This makes enums far more powerful than simple C-style enums.

lessons.title: Using Enums
lessons.body: Create enum values by specifying the variant. You can define methods on enums with impl blocks, just like with structs.

lessons.title: Why Enums Matter
lessons.body: Enums enforce that you handle every possible case, preventing forgotten edge cases. They are central to Rust's type system and underpin important standard types like Option and Result. For a deeper dive, see The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) chapter on enums. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Pattern Matching
topics.description: Use match expressions to elegantly branch on values and destructure data.

lessons.title: The match Expression
lessons.body: A match expression compares a value against a series of patterns and runs the code for the first pattern that fits. Each arm uses => to separate the pattern from the code.

lessons.title: Exhaustive Matching
lessons.body: Rust requires that every match covers all possible values of the type. If you leave out a variant or value, the compiler will refuse to build. This prevents unhandled cases at compile time.

lessons.title: Wildcards and Bindings
lessons.body: The underscore _ acts as a catch-all that matches anything but ignores the value. You can also bind matched values to a variable to use them in the arm body.

lessons.title: Destructuring in Patterns
lessons.body: Patterns can pull apart structs, tuples, and enums to bind their inner values directly. This avoids manual field access and makes code concise. For a deeper dive, see The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) chapter on patterns and matching. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: The Option Type
topics.description: Safely represent the absence of a value with Option instead of null.

lessons.title: Some and None
lessons.body: Option<T> is an enum with two variants: Some(T) holds a value, and None represents no value. Rust has no null, so Option forces you to handle the missing-value case explicitly.
lessons.code:
let age: Option<u32> = Some(30);
let missing: Option<u32> = None;

lessons.title: Unwrapping Safely
lessons.body: Calling unwrap on an Option returns the inner value if it is Some, but panics if it is None. Use unwrap_or to provide a default value instead of risking a panic.

lessons.title: Transforming with map
lessons.body: The map method applies a function to the inner value of Some and returns a new Option. If the value is None, map returns None without calling the function.

lessons.title: Chaining with and_then
lessons.body: The and_then method is like map but the function itself returns an Option, avoiding nested Options. It is useful when each step in a chain might fail. For a deeper dive, see The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) chapter on the Option type. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Result & Error Handling
topics.description: Handle operations that can fail using Result, Ok, Err, and the ? operator.

lessons.title: Ok and Err
lessons.body: Result<T, E> is an enum with Ok(T) for success and Err(E) for failure. Functions that can fail return Result so callers must handle both outcomes explicitly.

lessons.title: Matching on Result
lessons.body: You can use match to handle Ok and Err separately. This gives you full control over what happens on success and failure.

lessons.title: The ? Operator
lessons.body: The ? operator placed after a Result expression returns the Ok value if successful, or immediately returns the Err from the current function. It drastically reduces boilerplate for chaining fallible operations.

lessons.title: Practical Error Handling Tips
lessons.body: For small programs, returning Result with a boxed error trait object keeps things simple. In libraries, define custom error types for clarity. Avoid unwrap in production code because it panics on errors. For a deeper dive, see The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) chapter on error handling. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Vectors
topics.description: Store and manipulate growable lists of values with Vec<T>.

lessons.title: Creating Vectors
lessons.body: Vec<T> is a growable array stored on the heap. You can create one with Vec::new or the vec! macro. The macro lets you provide initial values conveniently.
lessons.code:
let mut nums: Vec<i32> = Vec::new();
let colors = vec!["red", "green", "blue"];

lessons.title: Push and Pop
lessons.body: Use push to add an element to the end and pop to remove the last element. Pop returns an Option because the vector might be empty.

lessons.title: Indexing and get
lessons.body: Access elements by index with brackets, which panics on out-of-bounds, or with get, which returns an Option. Prefer get when the index might be invalid.

lessons.title: Iterating over Vectors
lessons.body: Use a for loop to iterate over references to each element. You can also iterate mutably with iter_mut to modify elements in place. For a deeper dive, see The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) chapter on vectors. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: HashMaps
topics.description: Store key-value pairs for fast lookups using HashMap.

lessons.title: Creating a HashMap
lessons.body: A HashMap<K, V> stores associations between keys and values. Import it from std::collections. Create one with HashMap::new and add entries with insert.

lessons.title: Reading Values with get
lessons.body: Use get with a key reference to look up a value. It returns Option<&V>, so you get None if the key is missing rather than a panic.

lessons.title: Updating Entries
lessons.body: Calling insert with an existing key overwrites the value. Use entry and or_insert to insert only if the key does not already exist. This pattern is common for building counters.

lessons.title: Iterating over a HashMap
lessons.body: Use a for loop to iterate over key-value pairs as references. The order of iteration is arbitrary because HashMap does not maintain insertion order. For a deeper dive, see The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) chapter on hash maps. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: String Slices
topics.description: Understand the difference between String and &str and how to slice text safely.

lessons.title: String vs &str
lessons.body: String is an owned, heap-allocated, growable string. The type &str is a borrowed reference to a string slice, which is a view into string data. String literals are &str by default.

lessons.title: Slicing Strings
lessons.body: You can take a substring by indexing with a byte range. Be careful: the range must land on valid UTF-8 character boundaries or your program will panic at runtime.

lessons.title: Common String Methods
lessons.body: Both String and &str offer useful methods like contains, starts_with, trim, and to_uppercase. Methods that need to allocate return a new String, while read-only checks work on &str directly.

lessons.title: Converting Between String and &str
lessons.body: Convert &str to String with to_string, String::from, or to_owned. Go the other way by borrowing with & or calling as_str. Understanding when to own and when to borrow is key to writing efficient Rust. For a deeper dive, see The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) chapter on strings. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Modules & Visibility
topics.description: Organize code into modules and control what is public or private.

lessons.title: Defining Modules
lessons.body: Use the mod keyword to define a module inside a file. Modules create separate namespaces and can be nested. Items inside a module are private by default.

lessons.title: The pub Keyword
lessons.body: Mark an item with pub to make it accessible outside its module. Without pub, functions, structs, and constants are only visible within the same module and its children.

lessons.title: The use Keyword
lessons.body: The use statement brings a path into scope so you can refer to items by their short name. You can also rename imports with as to avoid name conflicts.

lessons.title: File-Based Module Structure
lessons.body: In larger projects, each module lives in its own file. A mod statement in the parent file tells the compiler to load the child file. A directory with a mod.rs file defines a module with submodules. For a deeper dive, see The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) chapter on modules. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Method Syntax
topics.description: Attach behavior to your types using impl blocks, self, and associated functions.

lessons.title: Implementing Methods
lessons.body: Define methods for a type inside an impl block. Methods take self as the first parameter, which refers to the instance the method is called on.

lessons.title: Self Variants
lessons.body: Use &self to borrow immutably, &mut self to borrow mutably, and self to take ownership. Most methods use &self because they only need to read the data.

lessons.title: Associated Functions
lessons.body: Functions inside an impl block that do not take self are called associated functions. They are often used as constructors. Call them with the double-colon syntax.

lessons.title: Multiple impl Blocks
lessons.body: You can split methods across several impl blocks for the same type. This is especially handy when implementing traits separately from inherent methods. For a deeper dive, see The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) chapter on method syntax. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: if let & while let
topics.description: Use concise pattern matching alternatives when you only care about one variant.

lessons.title: The if let Shorthand
lessons.body: When you only care about one match arm, if let is cleaner than a full match. It binds the value if the pattern matches and skips the block otherwise.

lessons.title: if let with else
lessons.body: Add an else branch to handle the non-matching case. This is equivalent to a match with a wildcard arm, but more concise when there are only two outcomes.

lessons.title: The while let Loop
lessons.body: while let repeatedly matches a pattern and runs the loop body as long as it succeeds. It is commonly used to drain a collection one element at a time.

lessons.title: When to Choose if let vs match
lessons.body: Use if let when you only need to act on one variant and want concise code. Use match when you need to handle all variants or more than two cases. Both are pattern matching under the hood. For a deeper dive, see The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) chapter on if let control flow. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Iterators Basics
topics.description: Traverse and transform collections lazily using Rust's iterator trait.

lessons.title: What is an Iterator?
lessons.body: An iterator produces a sequence of values one at a time. In Rust, any type implementing the Iterator trait has a next method that returns Some(value) until exhausted, then None.

lessons.title: iter, into_iter, and iter_mut
lessons.body: iter borrows each element immutably. iter_mut borrows each element mutably. into_iter takes ownership of the collection and yields owned values. Choose based on whether you need to keep the original collection.

lessons.title: Common Adapters
lessons.body: Adapters like map, filter, and take transform an iterator into a new iterator lazily. Nothing happens until you consume the iterator with a method like collect or for_each.

lessons.title: Consuming with collect and sum
lessons.body: The collect method gathers iterator results into a collection. The sum and product methods reduce numeric iterators to a single value. Turbofish syntax tells Rust the desired output type. For a deeper dive, see The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) chapter on iterators. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Closures Essentials
topics.description: Write anonymous functions that capture variables from their environment.

lessons.title: Closure Syntax
lessons.body: Closures are anonymous functions defined with pipes for parameters. The compiler infers parameter and return types in most cases. Braces are optional for single-expression bodies.

lessons.title: Capturing Variables
lessons.body: Closures can capture variables from the surrounding scope. By default, Rust decides whether to borrow immutably, borrow mutably, or take ownership based on how the variable is used inside the closure.

lessons.title: The move Keyword
lessons.body: Use move before the parameter list to force the closure to take ownership of captured variables. This is essential when passing closures to new threads or returning them from functions.

lessons.title: Fn, FnMut, and FnOnce Traits
lessons.body: Every closure implements one or more of the Fn traits. Fn borrows immutably, FnMut borrows mutably, and FnOnce consumes captured values. Function signatures accepting closures use these traits as bounds. For a deeper dive, see The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) chapter on closures. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Reading User Input
topics.description: Read text from the terminal using std::io and parse it into useful types.

lessons.title: Standard Input Basics
lessons.body: Rust provides std::io::stdin to read from the terminal. Call read_line to capture user input into a mutable String. The method appends a newline, so trim the result.

lessons.title: Parsing Input into Numbers
lessons.body: User input arrives as a string. Use parse to convert it to a numeric type. Handle the Result to avoid panics when the user types something unexpected.

lessons.title: Reading in a Loop
lessons.body: Wrap read_line in a loop to repeatedly prompt the user. Break out of the loop when you receive valid input or a sentinel value like an empty line.

lessons.title: Buffered Reading with BufRead
lessons.body: For reading multiple lines efficiently, use the BufRead trait and its lines method. It returns an iterator of Result values, one per line, automatically stripping newlines. For a deeper dive, see The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) chapter on I/O. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Basic Testing
topics.description: Write and run tests with the built-in test framework and cargo test.

lessons.title: Writing Your First Test
lessons.body: Annotate a function with #[test] to make it a test. The function should have no parameters and no return value. Cargo discovers and runs all #[test] functions automatically.

lessons.title: assert!, assert_eq!, and assert_ne!
lessons.body: assert! checks that a boolean expression is true. assert_eq! checks that two values are equal, and assert_ne! checks they are not. On failure, they print the values to help you debug.

lessons.title: Running Tests with cargo test
lessons.body: Run cargo test in your project directory to compile and execute all tests. Cargo shows a pass or fail summary. Use cargo test test_name to run a specific test by name.

lessons.title: Testing for Panics and Organizing Tests
lessons.body: Use the #[should_panic] attribute to verify that a function panics as expected. Group tests inside a mod tests block with #[cfg(test)] so they compile only during testing. For a deeper dive, see The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) chapter on testing. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Traits & Implementation
topics.description: Define shared behavior across types using traits and implement them for your own structs.

lessons.title: Defining a Trait
lessons.body: A trait defines a set of methods that a type must implement. Think of it as a contract: any type that implements the trait promises to provide those methods. Traits are Rust's primary tool for polymorphism.

lessons.title: Default Method Implementations
lessons.body: Traits can provide default method bodies. Types that implement the trait can use the default or override it. This reduces boilerplate when many types share the same logic.

lessons.title: Trait Bounds on Functions
lessons.body: You can require function parameters to implement specific traits using the impl Trait syntax or explicit bounds. This lets you write generic code that works with any type meeting the contract.

lessons.title: Implementing Multiple Traits
lessons.body: A single type can implement many traits, and a single trait can be implemented by many types. This composability is what makes Rust's trait system powerful. For a deeper dive, consult The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) on traits. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Generics
topics.description: Write flexible and reusable code with generic types and functions in Rust.

lessons.title: Generic Functions
lessons.body: Generic functions work with any type by using a type parameter in angle brackets. The compiler generates specialized versions for each concrete type you use, so there is zero runtime cost.

lessons.title: Generic Structs
lessons.body: Structs can also hold generic types. This lets you create data structures that work with any type while remaining type-safe. Each field can use the same or different type parameters.

lessons.title: Generic Enums
lessons.body: Rust's most famous enums are generic: Option<T> and Result<T, E>. You can define your own generic enums to model flexible data variants that work across types.

lessons.title: Monomorphization
lessons.body: Rust compiles generics through monomorphization: it generates concrete code for every type you actually use. This means generic code runs as fast as hand-written specialized code. The tradeoff is slightly longer compile times but zero runtime overhead. For a deeper dive, consult The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) on generics. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Lifetimes
topics.description: Understand lifetime annotations and how Rust ensures references never outlive the data they point to.

lessons.title: Why Lifetimes Exist
lessons.body: Lifetimes are Rust's way of tracking how long references are valid. The compiler uses them to guarantee that you never have a dangling reference. Most of the time lifetimes are inferred, but sometimes you must annotate them explicitly.

lessons.title: Lifetime Annotations
lessons.body: Lifetime annotations use an apostrophe followed by a short name, like 'a. They tell the compiler how the lifetimes of multiple references relate to each other. Annotations don't change how long values live; they describe existing relationships.

lessons.title: Lifetime Elision Rules
lessons.body: Rust has three elision rules that let you skip lifetime annotations in common cases. If each parameter gets its own lifetime, if there is exactly one input lifetime it gets assigned to all outputs, and if one parameter is &self its lifetime is assigned to outputs. These rules cover most functions.

lessons.title: Lifetimes in Structs
lessons.body: When a struct holds a reference, it needs a lifetime annotation. This tells the compiler the struct cannot outlive the data it borrows. This pattern is common for lightweight views into larger data. For a deeper dive, consult The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) on lifetimes. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Trait Bounds & Where Clauses
topics.description: Constrain generic types precisely using trait bounds and where clauses for cleaner, safer code.

lessons.title: Basic Trait Bounds
lessons.body: Trait bounds restrict which types a generic function accepts. You write them after a colon on the type parameter. Only types that implement the required trait can be passed to the function.

lessons.title: Multiple Trait Bounds
lessons.body: A type parameter can require multiple traits using the + syntax. The type must implement all listed traits. This is useful when you need a combination of capabilities from a single value.

lessons.title: Where Clauses
lessons.body: When bounds get complex, move them to a where clause after the function signature. This keeps the function name and parameters readable. Where clauses are especially helpful when multiple type parameters each have several bounds.

lessons.title: Returning Bounded Types
lessons.body: You can use impl Trait as a return type to say the function returns some type implementing a trait without naming it. This is handy for returning closures or iterators. The compiler still knows the concrete type so there is no dynamic dispatch cost. For a deeper dive, consult The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) on trait bounds. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Box & Heap Allocation
topics.description: Use Box<T> to store data on the heap for recursive types, large values, and trait objects.

lessons.title: What Is Box<T>
lessons.body: Box<T> is the simplest smart pointer in Rust. It allocates data on the heap and stores a pointer on the stack. When the Box goes out of scope, both the pointer and the heap data are freed automatically.

lessons.title: When to Use the Heap
lessons.body: Use Box when you have a large value you don't want to copy on the stack, when you need a value with a size unknown at compile time, or when you want to transfer ownership without copying data. Stack allocation is faster, so prefer it unless you have a reason to use the heap.

lessons.title: Recursive Types with Box
lessons.body: Recursive types like linked lists need Box because the compiler must know the size of each type at compile time. Without Box, a recursive struct would be infinitely sized. Box provides the indirection that gives it a fixed size.

lessons.title: Box and Deref
lessons.body: Box implements the Deref trait, so you can treat a Box<T> just like a regular reference to T. The compiler automatically dereferences it when needed. This makes Box transparent in most code so you rarely need explicit dereferencing. For a deeper dive, consult The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) on smart pointers. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Rc & Reference Counting
topics.description: Share ownership of heap data between multiple parts of your program using Rc<T>.

lessons.title: Why Shared Ownership
lessons.body: Sometimes a value needs more than one owner. A node in a graph, for example, might be referenced by multiple edges. Rust's default single-ownership model doesn't allow this, so Rc<T> provides reference-counted shared ownership for single-threaded scenarios.

lessons.title: How Rc Works
lessons.body: Rc keeps a count of how many clones exist. Calling Rc::clone increments this count cheaply without copying the inner data. When each clone is dropped the count decrements, and the data is freed only when the count reaches zero.

lessons.title: Shared Lists with Rc
lessons.body: A classic use case is two lists sharing a common tail. Without Rc you would need to clone the tail data. With Rc both lists point to the same heap allocation and the data lives until both lists are dropped.

lessons.title: Rc Limitations
lessons.body: Rc only provides immutable access to the inner data. If you need to mutate shared data, pair it with RefCell for interior mutability. Rc also cannot be sent across threads; use Arc for thread-safe reference counting. For a deeper dive, consult The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) on Rc and reference counting. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Interior Mutability
topics.description: Mutate data even through shared references using RefCell<T> and Cell<T> for runtime borrow checking.

lessons.title: The Interior Mutability Pattern
lessons.body: Normally, Rust enforces borrowing rules at compile time. Interior mutability lets you mutate data even when there are immutable references to it by moving the borrow check to runtime. This is useful when you know your code is safe but the compiler cannot prove it.

lessons.title: RefCell<T> in Detail
lessons.body: RefCell tracks borrows at runtime. Calling borrow gives a shared reference, and borrow_mut gives a mutable one. If you violate borrowing rules at runtime, the program panics instead of producing undefined behavior. This trades compile-time safety for flexibility.

lessons.title: Combining Rc and RefCell
lessons.body: A common pattern is Rc<RefCell<T>> which gives you multiple owners that can all mutate the inner value. This is the Rust equivalent of a shared mutable variable, with safety enforced at runtime.

lessons.title: Cell<T> for Copy Types
lessons.body: Cell<T> is a simpler alternative for types that implement Copy. It lets you get and set values without borrowing. Since it copies the value in and out, there is no risk of aliased mutable references. Use Cell for simple counters or flags, and RefCell for complex types. For a deeper dive, consult The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) on interior mutability. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Error Handling Patterns
topics.description: Design robust error types using custom errors, the From trait, and idiomatic Rust patterns.

lessons.title: Custom Error Types
lessons.body: For libraries and larger programs, define your own error enum. Each variant represents a different failure mode. Implementing Display and the Error trait lets your error integrate with the standard error ecosystem.

lessons.title: The From Trait for Conversion
lessons.body: Implementing From lets the question mark operator automatically convert foreign errors into your custom type. This makes error propagation seamless: just use ? and Rust converts the error for you.

lessons.title: The ? Operator Chain
lessons.body: You can chain multiple fallible operations using the ? operator. Each call either succeeds and unwraps the value, or returns early with the error converted via From. This replaces verbose match chains with clean, linear code.

lessons.title: Choosing an Error Strategy
lessons.body: For quick scripts, Box<dyn Error> or anyhow-style erasure works well. For libraries, define a concrete error enum so callers can match on variants. The key is consistency: pick one strategy per crate and stick with it. For a deeper dive, consult The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) on error handling. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Iterators & Combinators
topics.description: Chain powerful iterator adaptors like map, filter, and fold to process collections expressively.

lessons.title: Iterator Basics
lessons.body: The Iterator trait requires one method: next, which returns Some(item) or None. Most collections provide iter, iter_mut, and into_iter to create iterators. Iterators are lazy: they do nothing until consumed.

lessons.title: Map and Filter
lessons.body: Map transforms each element by applying a closure. Filter keeps only elements where the closure returns true. Both are adaptors that return new iterators, so you can chain them without allocating intermediate collections.

lessons.title: Fold and Reduce
lessons.body: Fold accumulates all elements into a single value using an initial accumulator and a closure. It is the most general consumer: you can implement sum, product, and many other operations with fold.

lessons.title: Chaining and Collecting
lessons.body: Iterator chains compose naturally. You can chain multiple adaptors, then call collect to build any collection that implements FromIterator. Because adaptors are lazy, the entire chain runs in a single pass with no intermediate allocations. For a deeper dive, consult The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) on iterators. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Closures In Depth
topics.description: Master Fn, FnMut, and FnOnce traits to write flexible closures that capture their environment.

lessons.title: Closure Capture Modes
lessons.body: Closures capture variables from their environment in three ways: by reference (Fn), by mutable reference (FnMut), or by value (FnOnce). Rust infers the most permissive mode that works, but you can force ownership with the move keyword.

lessons.title: FnMut and FnOnce
lessons.body: FnMut closures mutate captured variables, so they need mutable access. FnOnce closures consume a captured value, so they can only be called once. The compiler picks the right trait based on what the closure body does.

lessons.title: Closures as Parameters
lessons.body: Functions can accept closures using generic bounds or impl Trait. Choose the most general trait that works: Fn if you only read, FnMut if you mutate, FnOnce if you consume. This gives callers maximum flexibility.

lessons.title: Returning Closures
lessons.body: To return a closure from a function, use impl Fn or Box<dyn Fn>. Since closures have unique anonymous types, you cannot name them directly. The impl Trait syntax works when the function always returns the same closure type; use Box for dynamic cases. For a deeper dive, consult The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) on closures. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Enums With Data
topics.description: Build expressive data models using Rust enums with associated data, methods, and state machines.

lessons.title: Enum Variants with Data
lessons.body: Rust enums can hold data in each variant, like tuples or structs. This makes them far more powerful than enums in most languages. Each variant can carry different types and amounts of data.

lessons.title: Methods on Enums
lessons.body: You can implement methods on enums just like structs. Use match inside methods to handle each variant. This keeps behavior close to the data it operates on.

lessons.title: State Machines with Enums
lessons.body: Enums naturally model state machines. Each variant is a state, and transitions are functions that consume one state and return another. The compiler ensures you handle every state, making illegal states unrepresentable.

lessons.title: Pattern Matching Power
lessons.body: Rust's pattern matching with enums supports destructuring, guards, nested patterns, and binding. Combined with exhaustive checking, this makes it nearly impossible to forget a case. Enums with data and match form one of Rust's most distinctive and powerful features. For a deeper dive, consult The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) on enums and pattern matching. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Type Aliases & Newtype
topics.description: Improve code clarity and type safety using type aliases and the newtype wrapper pattern.

lessons.title: Type Aliases
lessons.body: The type keyword creates an alias: a new name for an existing type. Aliases don't create new types; they just improve readability. They are especially useful for shortening long generic types like Result with a fixed error.

lessons.title: The Newtype Pattern
lessons.body: A newtype is a tuple struct with a single field. Unlike aliases, newtypes create distinct types that the compiler treats as different. This prevents mixing up values that have the same underlying type but different meanings.

lessons.title: Implementing Traits on Newtypes
lessons.body: Newtypes let you implement foreign traits on foreign types, bypassing the orphan rule. You wrap the foreign type in your newtype, then implement whatever traits you need on the wrapper.

lessons.title: When to Choose Each
lessons.body: Use type aliases when you only want shorter names and the compiler should treat both as the same type. Use newtypes when you want the compiler to enforce that two values with the same underlying type cannot be mixed up. Newtypes have zero runtime cost thanks to guaranteed same-layout optimization. For a deeper dive, consult The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) on type aliases and newtypes. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Working With Files
topics.description: Read, write, and manage files in Rust using std::fs, Path, and PathBuf.

lessons.title: Reading Files
lessons.body: The simplest way to read a file is std::fs::read_to_string, which loads the entire content into a String. For large files, use BufReader with a File to read line by line. Both methods return Result, so handle errors with ? or match.

lessons.title: Writing Files
lessons.body: Use fs::write to create or overwrite a file in one call. For appending or more control, open a File with OpenOptions. The Write trait provides write_all and write_fmt for formatted output.

lessons.title: Buffered Reading
lessons.body: BufReader wraps a File and buffers reads for efficiency. Combined with the lines iterator, you can process a file line by line without loading it all into memory. Each line is returned as a Result<String>.

lessons.title: Path and PathBuf
lessons.body: Path is an immutable reference to a file path, and PathBuf is its owned counterpart. They handle platform differences like separator characters automatically. Use them instead of raw strings for reliable cross-platform file operations. For a deeper dive, consult The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) on file I/O. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Collections Deep Dive
topics.description: Go beyond Vec and HashMap to explore VecDeque, BTreeMap, HashSet, and how to choose the right collection.

lessons.title: VecDeque for Double-Ended Access
lessons.body: VecDeque is a double-ended queue built on a ring buffer. It offers O(1) push and pop from both front and back, unlike Vec which is O(n) for front operations. Use it when you need a queue or need to insert at both ends.

lessons.title: BTreeMap for Sorted Keys
lessons.body: BTreeMap stores entries sorted by key using a B-tree. Lookups are O(log n) instead of HashMap's O(1), but you get sorted iteration and range queries. Use it when you need ordered keys or your key type doesn't implement Hash.

lessons.title: HashSet for Unique Values
lessons.body: HashSet stores unique values with O(1) lookup. It supports set operations like union, intersection, and difference. Use it when you need to check membership or eliminate duplicates efficiently.

lessons.title: Choosing the Right Collection
lessons.body: Use Vec for ordered sequences with fast indexing. Use HashMap or HashSet for fast key lookups. Use BTreeMap or BTreeSet when you need sorted order. Use VecDeque for FIFO queues or double-ended access. The right collection depends on your access patterns, not just the data shape. For a deeper dive, consult The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) on collections. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Trait Objects & dyn
topics.description: Use dynamic dispatch with dyn Trait to work with different types through a common interface at runtime.

lessons.title: What Are Trait Objects
lessons.body: A trait object lets you store different concrete types behind a single interface. You create one by using dyn Trait behind a reference or smart pointer. The compiler uses a vtable for dynamic dispatch, resolving method calls at runtime instead of compile time.

lessons.title: Using dyn Trait
lessons.body: You can collect different types in a Vec<Box<dyn Trait>> or accept them as function parameters with &dyn Trait. This is useful when the set of types isn't known at compile time, like plugin systems or heterogeneous collections.

lessons.title: Object Safety
lessons.body: Not every trait can be used as a trait object. A trait is object-safe if its methods don't return Self and don't use generics. This is because the compiler needs a fixed vtable layout. If a trait isn't object-safe, use generics with trait bounds instead.

lessons.title: Generics vs Trait Objects
lessons.body: Generics use static dispatch with monomorphization, producing fast specialized code but larger binaries. Trait objects use dynamic dispatch with a small runtime cost but keep binary size down. Choose generics when performance is critical and types are known at compile time; choose trait objects when you need runtime flexibility. For a deeper dive, consult The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) on trait objects and dynamic dispatch. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Concurrency With Threads
topics.description: Harness native OS threads in Rust using std::thread, spawn, join, and move closures for safe parallel execution.

lessons.title: Spawning Threads
lessons.body: Rust provides native OS threads via std::thread::spawn. Each call to spawn creates a new thread that runs a closure concurrently with the main thread. The function returns a JoinHandle that lets you wait for the thread to finish.

lessons.title: Move Closures In Threads
lessons.body: By default, closures borrow variables from the enclosing scope. When you pass a closure to thread::spawn, the compiler cannot guarantee the borrowed data will outlive the thread. Using the move keyword transfers ownership of captured variables into the closure so the new thread owns its data independently.

lessons.title: Joining Multiple Threads
lessons.body: You can spawn many threads and collect their JoinHandles into a vector. Iterating over the handles and calling join on each ensures all threads complete before the program continues. This pattern is common when splitting a workload across threads.

lessons.title: Thread Panics And Safety
lessons.body: If a spawned thread panics, the panic is captured inside the JoinHandle. Calling join returns a Result, and you can inspect or propagate the error. This design prevents one thread from silently crashing the whole program. For a deeper dive, consult The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) on fearless concurrency. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Message Passing
topics.description: Use mpsc channels to send data between threads following Rust's philosophy of sharing memory by communicating.

lessons.title: Creating A Channel
lessons.body: The std::sync::mpsc module provides a multi-producer, single-consumer channel. Calling mpsc::channel returns a Sender and Receiver pair. Data sent through the Sender is received in FIFO order by the Receiver.

lessons.title: Channels Across Threads
lessons.body: Channels shine when communicating between threads. Move the Sender into a spawned thread and receive on the main thread. The recv call blocks until a message arrives or all senders are dropped.

lessons.title: Multiple Producers
lessons.body: Clone the Sender to give multiple threads their own handle to the same channel. Each clone is an independent producer. The single Receiver collects messages from all producers in arrival order.

lessons.title: Channel Patterns And Backpressure
lessons.body: Use mpsc::sync_channel to create a bounded channel that blocks the sender when the buffer is full, providing natural backpressure. Unbounded channels created with mpsc::channel can grow without limit, so choose based on your workload. For a deeper dive, consult The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) on message passing. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Shared State Concurrency
topics.description: Safely share mutable data across threads using Mutex<T> and Arc<T> while understanding deadlock risks.

lessons.title: Mutex Basics
lessons.body: A Mutex wraps data and enforces exclusive access through locking. You call lock to obtain a MutexGuard, which implements Deref so you can use it like a reference. The guard automatically releases the lock when it goes out of scope.

lessons.title: Sharing A Mutex With Arc
lessons.body: Rc is not thread-safe, so Rust provides Arc (atomic reference counting) for shared ownership across threads. Wrap a Mutex in an Arc, clone the Arc for each thread, and each thread can lock the mutex independently.

lessons.title: Deadlock Awareness
lessons.body: A deadlock occurs when two or more threads each hold a lock and wait for the other to release theirs. Rust prevents data races at compile time but cannot prevent deadlocks. Always acquire locks in a consistent order and keep critical sections short to minimize risk.

lessons.title: RwLock For Read-Heavy Workloads
lessons.body: When many threads read data but writes are rare, RwLock allows multiple concurrent readers or one exclusive writer. This improves throughput compared to Mutex for read-heavy access patterns. Choose Mutex for simplicity and RwLock when profiling shows read contention. For a deeper dive, consult The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) on shared-state concurrency. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Async & Await
topics.description: Write non-blocking concurrent code with async functions, the .await keyword, and futures in Rust.

lessons.title: Async Functions And Futures
lessons.body: An async fn returns a Future instead of executing immediately. The function body only runs when the future is polled by an async runtime. This lazy evaluation model lets you compose asynchronous operations efficiently.

lessons.title: Using .await
lessons.body: Inside an async context, call .await on a future to suspend the current task until the future resolves. While suspended, the runtime can execute other tasks. This cooperative scheduling achieves concurrency without threads for IO-bound work.

lessons.title: Running Futures With A Runtime
lessons.body: The standard library defines the Future trait but does not include a runtime. You need an executor like tokio or async-std to poll futures to completion. The block_on function is a common entry point that bridges synchronous main to the async world.

lessons.title: Concurrent Tasks With Join
lessons.body: Use join or select combinators to run multiple futures concurrently on the same thread. This is more lightweight than OS threads and ideal for IO-bound workloads. The runtime multiplexes tasks cooperatively at each await point. For a deeper dive, consult the Rust Async Book (rust-lang.github.io/async-book) and The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book). Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Unsafe Rust
topics.description: Understand when and how to use unsafe blocks, raw pointers, and unsafe functions to bypass compiler guarantees responsibly.

lessons.title: What Unsafe Unlocks
lessons.body: The unsafe keyword lets you perform five operations the compiler cannot verify: dereference raw pointers, call unsafe functions, access mutable statics, implement unsafe traits, and access union fields. Unsafe does not disable the borrow checker; it only permits these specific actions.

lessons.title: Unsafe Functions
lessons.body: Mark a function as unsafe fn when its contract includes invariants the caller must uphold. The caller wraps the call in an unsafe block, signaling they have verified the preconditions. This pushes responsibility to the programmer, not the compiler.

lessons.title: Safe Abstractions Over Unsafe Code
lessons.body: The best practice is to wrap unsafe operations inside safe public APIs. The standard library does this extensively. For example, Vec uses unsafe internally for memory management but exposes a fully safe interface. Keep unsafe blocks minimal and well-documented.

lessons.title: When To Use Unsafe
lessons.body: Reach for unsafe only when you need to interface with hardware, call C libraries, or implement data structures the borrow checker cannot express. Always document the safety invariants. Incorrect unsafe code causes undefined behavior, so audit it carefully. For a deeper dive, consult the Rustonomicon (doc.rust-lang.org/nomicon) which covers unsafe Rust in exhaustive detail. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Macros: macro_rules!
topics.description: Write declarative macros with macro_rules! to generate repetitive code using pattern matching and repetitions.

lessons.title: Your First Macro
lessons.body: Declarative macros match against source code patterns and expand into new code at compile time. The macro_rules! construct defines a macro with one or more arms. Each arm has a matcher and a transcriber separated by =>.

lessons.title: Capturing Fragments
lessons.body: Matchers use designators like $name:expr, $t:ty, or $id:ident to capture fragments of Rust syntax. The captured fragment is substituted in the transcriber wherever $name appears. This lets macros work with arbitrary expressions and types.

lessons.title: Repetitions
lessons.body: Use $(...),* for zero-or-more repetitions or $(...),+ for one-or-more. Repetitions let a single macro arm accept a variable number of arguments. This is how the standard vec! macro accepts any number of elements.

lessons.title: Macro Hygiene And Limits
lessons.body: Rust macros are partially hygienic: local variables inside macros do not leak into the caller scope. However, declarative macros cannot generate new identifiers dynamically or operate on types at a semantic level. When you hit these limits, consider procedural macros instead. For a deeper dive, consult The Rust Reference (doc.rust-lang.org/reference/macros-by-example.html) on macros by example. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Procedural Macros
topics.description: Explore derive macros, attribute macros, and function-like procedural macros that operate on Rust token streams.

lessons.title: What Are Procedural Macros
lessons.body: Procedural macros are Rust functions that receive a TokenStream and return a TokenStream. They run at compile time and can inspect or transform code structurally. Unlike declarative macros, they are defined in a separate crate with proc-macro = true in Cargo.toml.

lessons.title: Derive Macros
lessons.body: Derive macros generate impl blocks automatically when you annotate a struct or enum with #[derive(YourMacro)]. The macro receives the type definition as tokens and produces the implementation. The syn and quote crates make parsing and generating tokens ergonomic.

lessons.title: Attribute Macros
lessons.body: Attribute macros attach to items like functions or structs via #[your_attr]. They receive two token streams: the attribute arguments and the annotated item. This gives you full control to rewrite or wrap the item.

lessons.title: When To Use Proc Macros
lessons.body: Procedural macros shine for serialization, ORM mapping, CLI argument parsing, and API boilerplate. They add compile time cost, so prefer declarative macros or generics when they suffice. Keep macro logic in a dedicated crate and write tests with trybuild or macrotest. For a deeper dive, consult The Rust Reference (doc.rust-lang.org/reference/procedural-macros.html) on procedural macros. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Advanced Traits
topics.description: Master associated types, operator overloading, supertraits, and fully qualified syntax for complex trait designs.

lessons.title: Associated Types
lessons.body: Associated types let a trait define a placeholder type that implementors choose. Unlike generics on the trait, each type can implement the trait only once, making the API cleaner. The Iterator trait uses this pattern with its Item associated type.

lessons.title: Operator Overloading
lessons.body: Rust lets you overload operators by implementing traits from std::ops. For example, implementing Add lets you use the + operator on your type. The Output associated type determines the result type of the operation.

lessons.title: Supertraits
lessons.body: A supertrait is a trait that requires another trait as a prerequisite. This lets you call methods from the parent trait inside your trait's default implementations or blanket impls. Use the syntax trait Child: Parent to declare the relationship.

lessons.title: Fully Qualified Syntax
lessons.body: When a type implements multiple traits with methods of the same name, use fully qualified syntax to disambiguate. The form <Type as Trait>::method(&instance) tells the compiler exactly which implementation to call. This also works for associated functions that take no self parameter. For a deeper dive, consult The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) on advanced traits. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Advanced Lifetimes
topics.description: Explore lifetime subtyping, higher-ranked trait bounds, and the 'static lifetime for complex borrow relationships.

lessons.title: Lifetime Subtyping
lessons.body: Lifetime subtyping lets you express that one lifetime outlives another. The syntax 'a: 'b means 'a lives at least as long as 'b. This is needed when a struct holds references with different lifetimes and you must prove the relationship to the compiler.

lessons.title: Higher-Ranked Trait Bounds
lessons.body: Higher-ranked trait bounds (HRTBs) use the syntax for<'a> to say a trait must hold for all possible lifetimes. This is common with closures that accept references. The compiler infers HRTBs in many cases, but you sometimes need to write them explicitly.

lessons.title: The 'static Lifetime
lessons.body: The 'static lifetime means the reference can live for the entire program duration. String literals are 'static because they are baked into the binary. A bound T: 'static means T contains no non-static references, not that it lives forever. Owned types like String satisfy T: 'static.

lessons.title: Lifetime Elision Revisited
lessons.body: The compiler applies three elision rules to infer lifetimes in function signatures. When the rules are insufficient the compiler asks you to annotate explicitly. Understanding elision helps you write cleaner signatures and recognize when manual annotation is truly needed. For a deeper dive, consult The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) on advanced lifetimes and the Rustonomicon (doc.rust-lang.org/nomicon) for edge cases. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Smart Pointers Overview
topics.description: Compare Box, Rc, Arc, and RefCell to understand when to use each smart pointer for memory management.

lessons.title: Box For Heap Allocation
lessons.body: Box<T> allocates data on the heap with a single owner. It is the simplest smart pointer and costs nothing beyond the allocation. Use Box for recursive types, large data you want off the stack, or trait objects.

lessons.title: Rc For Shared Ownership
lessons.body: Rc<T> enables multiple owners of the same heap data in a single thread via reference counting. Cloning an Rc increments the count; dropping one decrements it. The data is freed when the count reaches zero. Rc does not allow mutation by default.

lessons.title: Arc For Thread-Safe Sharing
lessons.body: Arc<T> is the atomic version of Rc, safe to share across threads. It uses atomic operations for the reference count, which adds slight overhead. Pair Arc with Mutex or RwLock when you need shared mutable state across threads.

lessons.title: RefCell For Interior Mutability
lessons.body: RefCell<T> enforces borrowing rules at runtime instead of compile time. You can obtain a mutable reference even when the RefCell itself is behind a shared reference. If you violate the rules at runtime, RefCell panics. Combine Rc<RefCell<T>> for shared mutable single-threaded data. For a deeper dive, consult The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) on smart pointers. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: PhantomData & Zero-Sized Types
topics.description: Use PhantomData and zero-sized types to encode type-level information without runtime cost.

lessons.title: Zero-Sized Types
lessons.body: A zero-sized type (ZST) occupies no memory at runtime. Unit structs and empty enums are ZSTs. The compiler optimizes them away entirely. ZSTs are useful as marker types, map values you do not need, or type-level tags.

lessons.title: PhantomData Basics
lessons.body: PhantomData<T> is a ZST from std::marker that tells the compiler your type logically owns or references T even though no field actually stores it. This affects drop ordering, variance, and auto-trait implementations without adding runtime size.

lessons.title: Typestate With PhantomData
lessons.body: PhantomData enables the typestate pattern where a struct's generic parameter represents its state. Transitions between states are methods that consume self and return a new type. The compiler enforces valid state transitions at zero runtime cost.

lessons.title: Variance And Drop Check
