lessons.body: PhantomData influences how the compiler infers variance and drop check for your type. Using PhantomData<T> implies ownership, so the compiler may require T to outlive your struct. Use PhantomData<fn() -> T> for covariance without implying ownership. Getting this right matters for unsafe code. For a deeper dive, consult the Rustonomicon (doc.rust-lang.org/nomicon) on PhantomData and subtyping. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Pin & Unpin
topics.description: Understand pinning, self-referential structs, and why async Rust relies on Pin<T> to guarantee memory safety.

lessons.title: The Problem Pin Solves
lessons.body:
Some types contain internal pointers to their own fields, making them self-referential. If such a value is moved in memory, the internal pointers become dangling. Pin

is a wrapper that prevents the pointee from being moved, making self-referential types safe.

lessons.title: Pin And Unpin
lessons.body: Most Rust types implement the auto trait Unpin, meaning they are safe to move even when pinned. Pin only restricts types that do NOT implement Unpin. Futures generated by async blocks are !Unpin because they may be self-referential across await points.

lessons.title: Pinning On The Heap
lessons.body: Box::pin places a value on the heap and returns Pin<Box<T>>. Since the heap address is stable, the value is effectively immovable. This is the easiest way to pin a future or self-referential struct.

lessons.title: Pin In Async Runtimes
lessons.body: Async runtimes must pin futures before polling them because the Future::poll method requires Pin<&mut Self>. This contract ensures that a future's internal state is never moved between polls. Understanding Pin is essential for writing low-level async combinators and executors. For a deeper dive, consult the Rustonomicon (doc.rust-lang.org/nomicon) on pinning and the Rust Async Book (rust-lang.github.io/async-book). Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Custom Iterators
topics.description: Implement the Iterator and IntoIterator traits to create lazy, composable data pipelines for your own types.

lessons.title: Implementing Iterator
lessons.body: To make a type iterable, implement the Iterator trait which requires a type Item and a next method. The next method returns Some(Item) while there are elements and None when exhausted. This enables your type to work with for loops and all iterator adapters.

lessons.title: Using Your Iterator
lessons.body: Once you implement Iterator, you get dozens of adapter methods for free: map, filter, take, zip, collect, and more. These adapters are lazy and compose without allocating intermediate collections. Only a terminal operation like collect or for_each drives evaluation.

lessons.title: IntoIterator
lessons.body: The IntoIterator trait converts a type into an iterator. Implementing it lets your type be used directly in for loops. Typically you implement IntoIterator for references to your collection and for the owned type, mirroring how Vec works.

lessons.title: Lazy Evaluation And Performance
lessons.body: Rust iterators compile down to the same machine code as hand-written loops thanks to zero-cost abstractions. The compiler inlines and fuses adapter chains, eliminating intermediate allocations. This makes iterator pipelines both expressive and performant in production code. For a deeper dive, consult The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) on iterators and closures. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: FFI Basics
topics.description: Call C functions from Rust and expose Rust functions to C using extern, #[no_mangle], and FFI safety practices.

lessons.title: Calling C From Rust
lessons.body: Use an extern block with the "C" ABI to declare foreign functions. Calling them is unsafe because the compiler cannot verify the foreign code's safety guarantees. Link to the C library via Cargo build scripts or system linker flags.

lessons.title: Exposing Rust To C
lessons.body: To let C code call a Rust function, mark it with extern "C" and #[no_mangle]. The no_mangle attribute preserves the function name so C can find it by symbol. Set your crate type to cdylib or staticlib in Cargo.toml to produce a C-compatible library.

lessons.title: Working With C Strings
lessons.body: C strings are null-terminated byte arrays, while Rust strings are UTF-8 slices with a length. The std::ffi module provides CString for owned C strings and CStr for borrowed ones. Always handle the conversion carefully to avoid buffer overflows or invalid UTF-8.

lessons.title: FFI Safety Practices
lessons.body: Always wrap foreign calls in safe Rust functions that validate inputs and handle errors. Use the bindgen crate to auto-generate Rust bindings from C headers, reducing manual errors. Test FFI boundaries thoroughly, as bugs there cause undefined behavior. For a deeper dive, consult the Rustonomicon (doc.rust-lang.org/nomicon) on FFI and The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book). Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.

topics.title: Design Patterns In Rust
topics.description: Apply the builder pattern, typestate pattern, RAII, and the newtype idiom to write safe, expressive Rust APIs.

lessons.title: The Builder Pattern
lessons.body: The builder pattern constructs complex objects step by step. In Rust, the builder owns partial state and each method returns self for chaining. A final build method validates and produces the target type. This avoids constructors with many parameters.

lessons.title: The Typestate Pattern
lessons.body: The typestate pattern uses generics to encode an object's state in the type system. Methods are only available on the correct state, and transitions consume self and return a new state. This moves runtime checks to compile time.

lessons.title: RAII And Drop
lessons.body: Resource Acquisition Is Initialization ties resource management to object lifetime. When a value goes out of scope, its Drop implementation runs automatically, releasing files, locks, or memory. This eliminates resource leaks without manual cleanup calls.

lessons.title: The Newtype Pattern
lessons.body: Wrapping a primitive in a single-field tuple struct creates a distinct type that prevents accidental misuse. For example, Meters(f64) and Seconds(f64) cannot be mixed even though both hold f64. The compiler optimizes away the wrapper so there is no runtime cost. For a deeper dive, consult The Rust Programming Language book (doc.rust-lang.org/book) on patterns and idioms. Also visit the recommended books in the app for more insights.
